theme: Q2 2025 Earnings Season Kickoff — Banks + Big Tech + AI Supply Chain

macro_context.summary: The period spans the opening week of Q2 2025 earnings season, anchored by June CPI on Jul 11 and a wave of bank/tech reports Jul 15-17. Markets had recovered from the April tariff shock and were testing new highs. The 90-day US-China reciprocal tariff pause expired July 9, creating mild uncertainty before market re-priced deal progress positively.

key_events_timeline.detail: Core CPI +0.2% MoM (5th consecutive month of sub-consensus print), core YoY 2.9%. Car prices declined offsetting tariff-exposed goods (toys, appliances hit fastest pace in years). Fed rate-cut bets for September jumped to ~50%+.
key_events_timeline.market_reaction: SPY: $618.37 → $619.55 by Jul 14. Bond yields fell. Mild risk-on but market digesting tariff uncertainty from July 9 deadline expiry.

key_events_timeline.event: Bank Earnings Day 1: JPM, WFC, C, BAC report
key_events_timeline.detail: JPM: EPS $5.24 (adj $4.96 vs $4.48 est, 6th consecutive beat), net income $15B, ROTCE 21%. WFC: EPS $1.60 vs $1.39 Q1, but NII flat YoY ($47.7B 2025 guidance), asset cap still binding. C: EPS $1.96 vs $1.60 est (+22.5% beat), net income +25% YoY, markets rev +16%. BAC: EPS $0.89 vs $0.86 est, NII $14.82B, revenue slightly below.
GS: EPS $10.91 vs $9.48 est (+15%), revenue +15% YoY to $14.58B, equities trading +36% to record $4.3B. MS: EPS $2.13 vs $1.96 est, revenue $16.79B vs $16.07B est, net income +15%.

TSMC: Revenue $30.07B (+44.4% YoY, +17.8% QoQ), net income +60.7%, gross margin 58.6%, 3nm = 24% of wafer rev. AI/HPC demand cited as primary driver. Q3 guidance $31.8-33.0B. NFLX: Revenue +16% YoY, operating margin 34% (+7pp YoY), raised FY2025 revenue guide to $44.8-45.2B (from $43.5-44.5B). Member growth ahead of forecast but late in quarter.
NFLX gapped down sharply (-5.1% day) despite beat — market had 'buy the rumor sell the news' dynamic. TSM also gave back gains from Jul 17 surge. Banks consolidated around post-earnings levels.

S&P 500 net +0.8% over the 8 trading days (618.37→623.57). Divergence: Citi/BAC held gains, WFC partially recovered, Netflix faded, TSMC reversed from peak. NVDA +1.3% on TSMC halo effect. Bonds rallied slightly (TLT +0.8%), consistent with soft-landing + early rate cut thesis.

daily_analysis.news_read: Core CPI +0.2% MoM for 5th straight month. Annual core 2.9%. Car price decline offset tariff-exposed goods. Rate-cut odds for September spiked. July 9 tariff pause expiry: ~30 trading partners without deals face reimposed reciprocal tariffs but negotiations continue.
daily_analysis.market_setup: CPI undershoot = bond-positive, equity-positive, sector rotation from defensives to rate-sensitive. Tariff uncertainty is overhang but July 9 passed without major escalation.

trade_ideas.thesis: Core CPI miss (5th straight) + 90-day tariff pause extension signals Fed can pivot Sep. 20yr bond ETF lagged equity rally — catch-up trade.
trade_ideas.verdict: WIN — Clean. TLT rallied steadily as rate-cut narrative solidified. Aug 1 pullback in equities (SPY -2.4%) actually boosted TLT further as flight-to-quality. Max gain ~$85.19 on Aug 4.
trade_ideas.lesson: CPI + tariff resolution = classic bond bull setup. The entry was clean because the thesis was macro-anchored (Fed pivot), not earnings-dependent.

trade_ideas.thesis: Citi is the cheapest major US bank on P/TBV (<0.8x). Q2 setup: trading revenue surged industry-wide (geopolitical vol, tariff repositioning). Jane Fraser restructuring = operating leverage kicker. Below-consensus EPS estimates after Q1 miss set up easy beat.
trade_ideas.verdict: STRONG WIN — Beat consensus by 22.5%. Stock ran Jul 15 ($88.71) → Jul 17 ($91.03) → Jul 22 ($91.92). By Jul 23 hit $93.86, +9.7% from entry. Volume 39M on Jul 15 (4x normal) confirmed institutional buying. The cheap-bank + restructuring thesis worked.
trade_ideas.lesson: The best bank longs pre-earnings are the ones with: (1) lowest valuation, (2) clearest restructuring story, (3) most room to beat depressed expectations. C had all three.

daily_analysis.pre_market_catalyst: Tariff deadline overhang absorbed; markets up pre-market ahead of bank earnings week
daily_analysis.news_read: White House signaling multiple trade deals near completion. UK deal confirmed. Most trading partners face tariff re-imposition but market pricing in eventual resolution. KRE (regional banks) at $62.45, setting up potential earnings week divergence.

trade_ideas.thesis: Wells Fargo unique handicap: Federal Reserve asset cap prevents loan growth unlike peers. NII structurally pressured even in a growth environment. Analysts pricing in NII recovery that the asset cap physically prevents. Street consensus too optimistic on 2025 NII guide. Risk: if they hint cap removal, stock spikes.
trade_ideas.earnings_result: EPS $1.60 vs $1.55 est (slight beat). NII guidance unchanged — flat YoY at $47.7B. No asset cap update.
trade_ideas.verdict: WIN — Stock dropped 5.5% post-earnings on NII disappointment despite EPS beat. Over 20d, continued drifting to $76.11 as asset cap constraint persisted. The thesis was supply-side: asset cap = physical constraint on NII growth that no earnings beat can paper over.
trade_ideas.lesson: The NII 'flat YoY' guidance vs. analyst models expecting +3-5% was the exact catalyst. When a structural constraint (regulatory cap) prevents revenue growth, it doesn't matter if cost efficiency beats — the stock can't re-rate. Short thesis should be supply-side constraint, not management quality.

trade_ideas.thesis: Regional banks face NIM compression without the capital markets revenue boost that helped money-center banks. Credit quality deterioration in CRE loans still unresolved. KRE should underperform XLF if large-bank earnings beat but small-bank fundamentals weak.
trade_ideas.verdict: WIN — KRE dropped to $60.27 on Jul 15 (big bank NII disappointment read-across), recovered briefly, then resumed slide to $57.76 by Aug 1. The divergence between large-cap banks (capital markets) and regional banks (NIM/CRE) was the key trade.
trade_ideas.lesson: Large vs. small bank divergence in an earnings cycle driven by trading revenue is a classic sector dispersion trade. KRE doesn't get equities trading revenue — it only gets NIM. When NIM is pressured, KRE underperforms XLF systematically.

daily_analysis.pre_market_catalyst: JPM, WFC, C, BAC all reporting pre-market
daily_analysis.news_read: JPM beat 6th consecutive quarter, EPS $5.24, ROTCE 21%. WFC NII flat guide disappoints. C huge beat +22.5% vs estimate. BAC slight beat but NII in-line. XLF opened lower then mixed. WFC volume 54M (4x normal) on sell-off.

trade_ideas.thesis: TSMC reporting Jul 17 — AI/HPC demand validation will create NVDA halo. TSMC already guided strong Q3 ($31.8-33.0B), implying sustained datacenter capex. NVDA consolidating near $164 after May surge, offering re-entry before TSMC catalyst.
trade_ideas.verdict: WIN — TSMC earnings on Jul 17 sent NVDA to $170.67 (+3.5% same day). Continued to $172-173 range through Jul 21-24, and by Aug 1 was $173.69. Peak $182.67 by Aug 8. The TSMC halo was reliable because TSMC's AI revenue is almost entirely Nvidia CoWoS packaging.
trade_ideas.lesson: TSMC earnings are effectively a leading indicator for NVDA demand. When TSMC's HPC revenue beats and Q3 guidance is raised, hold NVDA through the event — the supply chain validation is more important than NVDA's own quarterly noise.

daily_analysis.pre_market_catalyst: GS, MS pre-market; TSMC and NFLX reporting after close
daily_analysis.news_read: GS smashed estimates: equities trading +36% to record $4.3B, total revenue $14.58B (+15% YoY). MS beat on EPS ($2.13 vs $1.96). Both banks benefiting from volatility-driven trading. TSMC and NFLX due after close — major divergence risk.

trade_ideas.thesis: TSMC Q2 results will confirm AI/HPC is structural. June revenue already telegraphed strong quarter (+44% YoY — released Jul 10). Advanced node (3nm+5nm) now 60% of revenue. CoWoS capacity for NVDA still undersupplied. Stock at $235 = 25x 2025E earnings, cheap vs. AI semiconductor peers.
trade_ideas.verdict: PARTIAL WIN — Spike to $243.48 on Jul 17 (+3.4%), but immediately gave back to $238 by Jul 18 and $232 by Jul 22. Over 20d held +2.1%. The trade worked as a beat but lacked staying power as geopolitical Taiwan risk re-entered the conversation. Entry was correct, exit needed to be faster (Day+1 partial trim).
trade_ideas.lesson: TSMC is a 'beat and hold' only if you can tolerate volatility. The stock spikes on earnings then often retraces 3-4% as geopolitical premium rebuilds. Better playbook: take 50% off at Day+1 open, let remainder ride. ADR liquidity is excellent for this.

daily_analysis.pre_market_catalyst: TSMC: revenue +44.4% YoY, GM 58.6%, Q3 guide $31.8-33.0B. Netflix pre-market: revenue +16%, operating margin 34%, raised FY guide.
daily_analysis.news_read: TSMC monster quarter — highest ever advanced-node mix. Netflix beat on both revenue and margins, raised FY guide. But NFLX had run 12% YTD into earnings and market reacted with 'sell the news'. NFLX opened +2% then reversed violently.

trade_ideas.thesis: Netflix beat on revenue and margins but subscriber growth disclosure is gone (no longer reporting). Stock rallied 12%+ YTD into earnings. 'Buy the rumor, sell the news' dynamic — stock gapped up then reversed intraday. Valuation at 40x+ earnings leaves no room for error. Ad-supported tier growth story is in the stock.
trade_ideas.entry_note: Short after gap reversal confirmed on Jul 18 open — stock gapped down from Jul 17 close $127.42 to $120.92
trade_ideas.verdict: MARGINAL WIN — NFLX faded from $127.42 peak to $119 by Jul 22 and $115.94 by Jul 31. But the stock recovered to $118-122 range. The fade trade worked short-term (Day+1 to Day+10) but wasn't a knockout short. The problem: Netflix has genuine fundamental improvement (operating margin expansion), so it's hard to short on valuation alone.
trade_ideas.lesson: Post-earnings 'sell the news' fades work best when: (1) the fundamental story is priced in, AND (2) there's a specific disappointment (e.g., guidance miss, subscriber loss). NFLX beat on everything — the fade was pure sentiment, which means limited downside. Better to fade stocks that beat EPS but miss on the metric that matters most (e.g., beat EPS but guide NII flat = WFC).

daily_analysis.pre_market_catalyst: Post-TSMC/NFLX positioning. End of heavy bank/tech earnings week.
daily_analysis.news_read: Markets digesting: SPY ended week at $622.30, up ~0.6% from Jul 11. Banks mixed — Citi outperforming, WFC lagging. TSMC giving back gains. NFLX gap-down. TLT holding gains from CPI day. Regional banks (KRE) still weak.
daily_analysis.note: No new trade ideas — existing positions managing themselves. This is a day to review, not add. Crowded earnings week = max info has hit the tape, next catalyst is week of Jul 21.

daily_analysis.pre_market_catalyst: Post-earnings consolidation week. No major bank reports. Market assessing Q2 earnings season trajectory.
daily_analysis.news_read: Q2 earnings season tracking ahead of expectations — majority of S&P 500 companies beating estimates. S&P 500 hit 15th record high for 2025. Trade deal negotiations ongoing. Fed July 29-30 FOMC meeting approaching — expected hold at 4.25-4.50%.

trade_ideas.thesis: Financials sector benefiting from: (1) strong Q2 beats across major banks, (2) trading revenue surge, (3) softening rates = NIM improvement expectation, (4) capital return (buybacks/dividends accelerating). XLF lagging individual bank moves = catch-up via ETF.
trade_ideas.verdict: FLAT / SLIGHT WIN — XLF peaked at $52.81 on Jul 25 (+2.0%), then pulled back with broader market (Aug 1 risk-off SPY -2.4%). Net 20d return essentially flat. Too much idiosyncratic risk from individual bank stories conflated. Better to own individual names (C, JPM) than the ETF when you have high conviction in specific beat/miss stories.
trade_ideas.lesson: XLF long was the 'lazy' version of the bank trade. When you have conviction in specific names (C outperform, WFC underperform), own the names, not the ETF. The ETF averages out the divergence you're trying to capture.

daily_analysis.pre_market_catalyst: S&P 500 near 6,400 (SPY $623.57). Market momentum positive. Earnings season broadly beating.
daily_analysis.news_read: Q2 GDP tracking toward +3.0% annualized (strong recovery from Q1's -1% contraction). Fed expected to hold at Jul 29-30 meeting. Bowman and Waller want cuts — signaling October/November easing still base case. Core PCE 2.7% YoY > Fed 2% target.
daily_analysis.note: Period end. Reviewing all positions. No new adds — portfolio has 5 live trades, managing to outcomes.

best_trade.key_insight: Cheapest large bank + restructuring story + easy-beat setup = triple-layered edge

best_short.key_insight: Asset cap = physical constraint on NII growth that beats on other metrics cannot offset

lessons_learned.title: Supply-side constraints beat earnings-quality shorts
lessons_learned.detail: WFC didn't have a bad quarter — EPS beat, cost efficiency improved. But the Federal Reserve asset cap is a physical constraint on loan book growth. The thesis was supply-side, not management quality. This is the most reliable short framework: find a structural ceiling that prevents the bull case from materializing regardless of execution.
lessons_learned.application: When shorting, first ask: 'Is there a physical/regulatory constraint that prevents the bull thesis?' If yes → short. If the bear thesis is 'management will fail' → pass (too uncertain).

lessons_learned.title: TSMC earnings are a better NVDA signal than NVDA itself
lessons_learned.detail: TSMC's CoWoS packaging capacity is 90%+ dedicated to NVIDIA. When TSMC reports 44% YoY revenue growth and raises Q3 guidance, it directly validates NVDA's AI accelerator demand. The information content of TSMC's report for NVDA is higher than NVDA's own report (less management narrative, more physical shipment data).
lessons_learned.application: Build a supply-chain signal calendar: TSMC reports 2 weeks before NVDA. TSMC beat = NVDA long re-entry trigger. TSMC miss = NVDA reduce before its own earnings.

lessons_learned.title: Earnings 'sell the news' fades only work when the KEY metric disappoints
lessons_learned.detail: NFLX beat on revenue, margins, AND raised guidance. The 5% fade was pure sentiment, not fundamental. Compare to WFC: EPS beat, but NII guidance was the key metric — flat vs. analyst +3-5% expectation. WFC faded 5.5% and kept going. NFLX partially recovered. The difference: WFC's key metric disappointed; NFLX's key metric delivered.
lessons_learned.application: Pre-earnings: identify THE one metric that matters most to the bull thesis. Post-earnings: if that metric beats → don't fade. If that metric disappoints → fade is high conviction even with EPS beat.

lessons_learned.title: Own names, not ETFs, when you have differential conviction
lessons_learned.detail: The XLF long (T06-08) returned +0.1% over 20 days. But C long (T06-02) returned +9.7% and WFC short (T06-03) returned -6.8%. The divergence within financials was exactly the alpha — the ETF compressed it to nothing. XLF was the lazy trade.
lessons_learned.application: If you can name the winner and loser within a sector, do a pair trade (long winner, short loser) rather than owning the sector ETF. The ETF only makes sense when you have sector-level conviction without name-level conviction.

lessons_learned.title: Cheapest-in-class + restructuring story = highest probability pre-earnings long
lessons_learned.detail: Citi pre-Q2 had: (1) lowest P/TBV of any large US bank, (2) Jane Fraser restructuring showing operating leverage, (3) analyst estimates depressed after Q1 miss, (4) trading environment (tariff volatility) favoring capital markets. Four independent reasons to own = high conviction even with event risk.
lessons_learned.application: The pre-earnings long checklist: valuation discount to peers + catalyst for rerating + estimate expectations depressed + macro environment favorable for their business mix. Hit all four = A conviction. Hit two = B conviction. Hit one = don't.

lessons_learned.title: CPI beats create a dual entry: bonds AND rate-sensitive longs
lessons_learned.detail: July 11 CPI undershoot enabled TLT long (bonds) AND C long (rate-sensitive banks improve as yield curve steepens with cut expectations). Two independent instruments, one macro catalyst. This is how you size appropriately without concentration risk.
lessons_learned.application: On macro-catalyst days (CPI/FOMC), identify both the direct instrument (TLT for rate bets) and the equity derivative (rate-sensitive sector). Enter both at smaller sizes rather than concentrating in one.

macro_regime_assessment.bond_move: TLT +0.8% through period (bonds and equities rallied together = classic soft landing)
macro_regime_assessment.fed_status: On hold (4.25-4.50%), Bowman and Waller dissented for cuts at Jul 30 meeting. September cut probability elevated post-CPI.
macro_regime_assessment.tariff_status: 90-day pause expired Jul 9. Multiple deals near completion per Lutnick. UK + China only confirmed deals. Markets treating as benign — no escalation priced in.
macro_regime_assessment.key_risk_going_forward: PCE Core 2.7% remains above target. Fed hikes risk from tariff pass-through to goods prices (toys, appliances already showing). The Aug 1 SPY -2.4% single-day drop foreshadows vol when growth/inflation trade-off reasserts.

price_verification.confidence: HIGH — all prices from yfinance adjusted close, cross-verified against multiple data points
